objectives: Describe a wide range of emotions in Spanish
Use estar vs. ser for emotional states
Express empathy and give emotional support
Talk about mood and emotional reactions

dialogue_blocks.speaker: Patricia
dialogue_blocks.translation: You seem off today. Are you okay?

dialogue_blocks.speaker: Gabriela
dialogue_blocks.text: La verdad no. Estoy muy estresada con el trabajo.
dialogue_blocks.translation: Honestly, no. I'm very stressed about work.
dialogue_blocks.ipa: [la βeɾˈðað ˈno ‖ esˈtoj muj estɾeˈsaða kon el tɾaˈβaxo]

dialogue_blocks.speaker: Gabriela
dialogue_blocks.text: Mi jefe me criticó frente a todos y me sentí muy humillada.
dialogue_blocks.translation: My boss criticized me in front of everyone and I felt very humiliated.
dialogue_blocks.ipa: [mi ˈxefe me kɾitiˈko ˈfɾente a ˈtoðos i me senˈti muj umiˈʝaða]

dialogue_blocks.speaker: Patricia
dialogue_blocks.text: ¡Qué horrible! Eso no está bien. ¿Cómo te sientes ahora?
dialogue_blocks.translation: How horrible! That's not right. How do you feel now?
dialogue_blocks.ipa: [ke oˈriβle ‖ ˈeso no esˈta ˈβjen ‖ ˈkomo te ˈsjentes aˈoɾa]

dialogue_blocks.speaker: Gabriela
dialogue_blocks.text: Todavía me siento enojada y un poco triste. No sé qué hacer.
dialogue_blocks.translation: I'm still angry and a bit sad. I don't know what to do.
dialogue_blocks.ipa: [toˈðaβia me ˈsjento enoˈxaða i un ˈpoko ˈtɾiste]

dialogue_blocks.speaker: Patricia
dialogue_blocks.text: Tranquila. Respira y no te lo tomes tan personal.
dialogue_blocks.translation: Calm down. Breathe and don't take it so personally.
dialogue_blocks.ipa: [tɾaŋˈkila ‖ resˈpiɾa i no te lo ˈtomes tan peɾsoˈnal]

dialogue_blocks.speaker: Gabriela
dialogue_blocks.text: Tienes razón. Gracias por escucharme. Me siento mejor contigo.
dialogue_blocks.translation: You're right. Thanks for listening. I feel better with you.
dialogue_blocks.ipa: [ˈtjenes raˈθon ‖ ˈɡɾaθjas poɾ eskuˈʧaɾme ‖ me ˈsjento meˈxoɾ konˈtiɣo]

srs_flashcards.back: to be afraid / to have fear

exercises.question: Your friend just got a promotion at work. How would they most likely feel?

feedback.correct: ¡Correcto! Getting a promotion is exciting and happy news — 'feliz y emocionada'.
feedback.incorrect: A promotion is good news — the person would be 'feliz' (happy) and 'emocionada' (excited).

exercises.instruction: Une la emoción con su opuesto
Completa con la emoción correcta usando estar

dialogue.speaker: Mamá
dialogue.text: ¿Por qué _____ tan callada hoy? (estar — tú / triste)

dialogue.speaker: Hija
dialogue.text: _____ un poco triste. Tuve un problema con mi amiga. (estar — yo)

dialogue.speaker: Hija
dialogue.text: Creo que también _____ enojada conmigo. (estar — ella)

speaking_rubric.title: ¿Cómo te sientes? (How do you feel?)
speaking_rubric.scenario: A friend asks how you're doing. Describe your current mood and why — something positive or challenging that happened recently.
speaking_rubric.prompt: Describe cómo te sientes hoy y por qué. Usa verbos de emoción con estar.

sample_response.text: Hoy estoy un poco cansada porque trabajé mucho ayer. Pero también estoy contenta porque mañana tengo el día libre.
sample_response.ipa: [ˈoj esˈtoj un ˈpoko kanˈsaða ‖ ˈpeɾo tamˈbjen esˈtoj konˈtenta]

cultural_notes.formality: In Mexico, showing emotional support is important. Saying '¿Cómo estás de verdad?' (How are you really?) signals genuine concern beyond surface-level greetings.
cultural_notes.gestures: Placing a hand on someone's shoulder or arm while listening is a sign of genuine empathy in Mexican culture.
cultural_notes.regional_variations: 'Estar chido/a' (to feel good/cool) is very common Mexican slang. 'Estar agüitado/a' means to feel down or bummed out. These are casual but widely used.
